<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.CompleteResultConfig"></a>

## CompleteResultConfig Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CompleteResultConfig()
```

Server-supplied inputs to a ``CompleteResult``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.build_complete_result"></a>

#### build\_complete\_result

```python
def build_complete_result(config: CompleteResultConfig) -> dict
```

Build a successful ``CompleteResult`` (``resultType: "complete"``); optional fields only

when supplied. (§19.4)

**Raises**:

- `ValueError`: when more than 100 ``values`` are supplied. (R-19.4-c/-d)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.compute_completion"></a>

#### compute\_completion

```python
def compute_completion(ranked: list, *, total: int | None = None) -> dict
```

Reference engine: cap an already-ranked list at 100 and signal truncation. ``total``
defaults to ``len(ranked)``; ``hasMore`` is set when matches were dropped. (§19.4, R-19.4-c–-h)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.prefix_match"></a>

#### prefix\_match

```python
def prefix_match(seed: str,
                 candidates: list,
                 *,
                 case_insensitive: bool = False) -> list[str]
```

Return the ``candidates`` starting with ``seed`` (input order). Empty seed matches all.
The simplest SHOULD-permitted strategy; a server MAY substitute any matcher. (§19.5, R-19.5-d)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.build_completion_not_supported_error"></a>

#### build\_completion\_not\_supported\_error

```python
def build_completion_not_supported_error() -> dict
```

Build the -32601 error for ``completion/complete`` without the ``completions`` capability.
(§19.1, R-19.1-d)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.build_completion_invalid_params_error"></a>

#### build\_completion\_invalid\_params\_error

```python
def build_completion_invalid_params_error(detail: str) -> dict
```

Build a -32602 error for malformed ``completion/complete`` params. (§19.5, R-19.5-s)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.build_unknown_reference_error"></a>

#### build\_unknown\_reference\_error

```python
def build_unknown_reference_error(detail: str) -> dict
```

Build the -32602 error for an unknown ref / unknown argument (NOT a not-found result).
(§19.5, R-19.5-r)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.build_completion_internal_error"></a>

#### build\_completion\_internal\_error

```python
def build_completion_internal_error(detail: str | None = None) -> dict
```

Build the -32603 error for an internal completion failure. (§19.5, R-19.5-t)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.CompleteRequestValidation"></a>

## CompleteRequestValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CompleteRequestValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_complete_request`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.validate_complete_request"></a>

#### validate\_complete\_request

```python
def validate_complete_request(params: object) -> CompleteRequestValidation
```

Validate the SHAPE of ``completion/complete`` params (§19.2/§19.3), mapping failures to
-32602: ``ref`` REQUIRED + closed union, ``argument`` REQUIRED string name/value, and (when
present) ``context.arguments`` keys MUST NOT include ``argument.name``. The unknown-ref /
unknown-argument checks need the catalog — see :func:`resolve_completion_target`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.CompletionCatalog"></a>

## CompletionCatalog Objects

```python
class CompletionCatalog(Protocol)
```

The server's catalog used to detect an unknown ref / unknown argument. (R-19.5-r)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.CompletionTargetResolution"></a>

## CompletionTargetResolution Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CompletionTargetResolution()
```

Outcome of :func:`resolve_completion_target`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.resolve_completion_target"></a>

#### resolve\_completion\_target

```python
def resolve_completion_target(
        params: dict,
        catalog: CompletionCatalog) -> CompletionTargetResolution
```

Resolve a validated ``ref`` + ``argument.name`` against the server's catalog (R-19.5-r):
an unknown prompt / template, or an argument not part of the target, is rejected with
-32602 (NOT a not-found result).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.prompt_argument_names_of"></a>

#### prompt\_argument\_names\_of

```python
def prompt_argument_names_of(prompt: dict) -> list[str]
```

Return a prompt's declared argument names, for a :class:`CompletionCatalog`. (R-19.5-r)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.resource_template_variable_names_of"></a>

#### resource\_template\_variable\_names\_of

```python
def resource_template_variable_names_of(template: dict,
                                        extract_variables) -> list[str]
```

Return a template's URI-template variable names, using the caller-supplied extractor
(keep the §17.4 binding as the single source of template-variable parsing). (R-19.5-r)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.completion.create_completion_debouncer"></a>

#### create\_completion\_debouncer

```python
def create_completion_debouncer(
        run: Callable[[str], _T],
        wait_ms: int = 150) -> Callable[[str], "Future[_T]"]
```

Wrap a completion runner so rapid successive calls (one per keystroke) are coalesced into a

single in-flight ``completion/complete``: each call resets a ``wait_ms`` timer, and only the
final value after a quiet period is sent. All callers awaiting during a burst resolve with that
single result. (§19.5, R-19.5-n)

The thread-timer analogue of the TS ``setTimeout`` debouncer: each returned :class:`~concurrent.
futures.Future` is fulfilled when the coalesced ``run`` completes (or fails). ``run`` is invoked
off the calling thread on a :class:`threading.Timer`; a burst of calls within ``wait_ms`` fires
``run`` exactly once, with the LAST supplied value.

**Arguments**:

- `run`: Issues the actual ``completion/complete`` for an argument value.
- `wait_ms`: Quiet period (milliseconds) before the coalesced call fires. Default 150.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance"></a>

# stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance

Feature lifecycle status (§27, R-1.3-b, R-2.2-f – R-2.2-h).

A feature with status ``deprecated`` remains defined and MUST still be accepted by
receivers, but SHOULD NOT be relied upon by new implementations. This module owns
only the lifecycle-status vocabulary; the conformance requirements registry that
references it lives in :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance.FEATURE_STATUS_ACTIVE"></a>

#### FEATURE\_STATUS\_ACTIVE

A feature that is current and may be relied upon by new implementations.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance.FEATURE_STATUS_DEPRECATED"></a>

#### FEATURE\_STATUS\_DEPRECATED

A feature that remains defined and MUST still be accepted by receivers, but
SHOULD NOT be relied upon by new implementations. (§27, R-2.2-f – R-2.2-h)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance.FeatureStatus"></a>

## FeatureStatus Objects

```python
class FeatureStatus()
```

The feature lifecycle statuses, as a namespace of string constants.

Mirrors the TS ``FeatureStatus`` const object: ``FeatureStatus.Active`` /
``FeatureStatus.Deprecated`` map to the wire values ``"active"`` / ``"deprecated"``.
Exposed as class attributes so callers can write ``FeatureStatus.Active`` exactly
as the TS SDK does, while the module-level ``FEATURE_STATUS_*`` constants serve
idiomatic Python use. (§27)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance.FEATURE_STATUSES"></a>

#### FEATURE\_STATUSES

The full set of recognized feature lifecycle statuses, in spec order. (§27)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance.is_feature_status"></a>

#### is\_feature\_status

```python
def is_feature_status(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``value`` is a recognized :class:`FeatureStatus`. (§27)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements"></a>

# stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance\_requirements

S45 — Conformance Requirements & References (§29–§30).

The formal conformance contract: the precise, testable definition of what it means
for an MCP implementation to be conformant, decomposed along the three independent
axes of §29.1 — **role** (client / server / both), **feature surface** (baseline
plus whatever is advertised), and **transport** (each transport implemented,
independently). It restates, as a single machine-checkable rulebook, the baseline
obligations of every server and every client (§29.2, §29.3), the bidirectional
advertise⇔implement principle for capabilities and extensions (§29.4, §29.5), the
robustness rules for richer-than-understood inputs (§29.6), the stateless-model
invariants (§29.7), the transport obligations (§29.8), the method for determining
conformance (§29.9), and the provenance-only status of the §30 reference markers.

This is a conceptual, cross-cutting story: it defines NO new wire types. Its
artifacts are a registry of normative requirements (:data:`CONFORMANCE_REQUIREMENTS`),
a requirement-level classifier (:func:`classify_requirement_level`), the abstract
:class:`ConformanceProfile` descriptor and its validator
(:func:`validate_conformance_profile`), the baseline server request-disposition
predicate (:func:`classify_server_request`), the capability→obligation map
(:data:`CAPABILITY_OBLIGATIONS`), the robustness disposition
(:func:`robustness_disposition`), the stateless invariants
(:data:`STATELESS_CONFORMANCE_INVARIANTS`), the transport-conformance evaluator
(:func:`evaluate_transport_conformance`), and the §30 citation status
(:data:`CITATION_STATUS`).

REUSE (never redefined here):
  - ``RESULT_TYPE_*``, :func:`is_known_result_type`, :func:`interpret_result_type` —
    :mod:`stackific.mcp.jsonrpc.payload` (S04);
  - :data:`CURRENT_PROTOCOL_VERSION`, :func:`is_supported_protocol_version`,
    :func:`validate_request_meta`, the per-request ``_meta`` envelope keys —
    :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.meta` (S05);
  - :data:`UNSUPPORTED_PROTOCOL_VERSION_CODE`, :data:`MISSING_CLIENT_CAPABILITY_CODE`,
    :data:`INVALID_PARAMS_CODE` — :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.errors` (S34);
  - :func:`compute_missing_client_capabilities` — :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.capability_negotiation` (S10);
  - :func:`is_result_type_accepted` — :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.extension_mechanism` (S38);
  - :func:`is_valid_extension_id` — :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.extensions` (S38);
  - :data:`RECOGNIZED_INPUT_REQUEST_METHODS` — :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.multi_round_trip` (S17);
  - :class:`FeatureStatus` — :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance` (S43, NOT redefined).

The §35 transport-family authorization logic (HTTP vs stdio applicability and the
credential-conveyance mode) is mirrored locally as private helpers, since the
``stackific.mcp.protocol.authorization`` module is owned by another story; the behavior is
identical to S35's ``authorizationAppliesTo`` / ``authorizationForbiddenFor`` /
``credentialConveyanceFor`` (§23.1, R-23.1-a – R-23.1-c).

Out of scope (owned elsewhere, per the story §5): the definition of the error codes
and their ``data`` shapes (S34), ``server/discover`` mechanics (S08/S09), the
``_meta`` envelope / stateless-model definitions (S05/S06), the per-feature
MUST-level behaviors (S16–S31), the extension/Tasks/UI definitions (S38–S42),
deprecated features (S32/S33/S43), transport framing (S12–S15), the authorization
framework (S35–S37), and the consolidated registries (S46).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRole"></a>

#### ConformanceRole

A role an implementation plays. A requirement naming a role binds an
implementation only when it plays that role; an implementation playing BOTH
MUST satisfy each role's requirements. (§29.1, R-29.1-a, R-29.1-b)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceTransport"></a>

#### ConformanceTransport

A transport an implementation may implement. Open-ended (``str``) because §7's
transport set is extensible, but the two core transports are named.
(§29.8, R-29.8-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceAxis"></a>

#### ConformanceAxis

The three independent axes along which conformance is scoped. (§29.1) Conformance
is the product of these: an implementation is conformant iff every applicable
requirement on its chosen roles, advertised features, and implemented transports
is satisfied.

  - ``role``      — client / server / both (§29.1 item 1);
  - ``feature``   — baseline plus advertised capabilities/extensions (§29.1 item 2);
  - ``transport`` — each transport, independently (§29.1 item 3).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CONFORMANCE_AXES"></a>

#### CONFORMANCE\_AXES

The three conformance axes, in spec order. (§29.1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.RequirementLevel"></a>

#### RequirementLevel

A normative requirement level, in the RFC 2119 / RFC 8174 sense as established by
§2. The ``MAY``/``OPTIONAL``/``SHOULD`` family is conditionally applicable; the
``MUST`` family is unconditional. (§29.1, R-29.1-a)

  - ``MUST``   — also covers MUST NOT / REQUIRED / SHALL / SHALL NOT;
  - ``SHOULD`` — also covers SHOULD NOT / RECOMMENDED;
  - ``MAY``    — also covers OPTIONAL.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.REQUIREMENT_KEYWORDS"></a>

#### REQUIREMENT\_KEYWORDS

Every distinct RFC 2119 keyword recognized in the story's atoms, mapped to its
canonical :data:`RequirementLevel` family. (§2) The keys are the exact tokens the
story uses in its ``[R-… · KEYWORD]`` markers.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.RequirementKeyword"></a>

#### RequirementKeyword

A normative keyword as it appears in a requirement marker. (keys of
:data:`REQUIREMENT_KEYWORDS`)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.REQUIREMENT_LEVELS"></a>

#### REQUIREMENT\_LEVELS

The three requirement-level families, strongest first. (§2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.classify_requirement_level"></a>

#### classify\_requirement\_level

```python
def classify_requirement_level(keyword: str) -> RequirementLevel | None
```

Classify a normative ``keyword`` into its :data:`RequirementLevel` family. (§2)

Returns ``None`` for an unrecognized token — never raises, so a conformance
harness can report rather than crash on a malformed marker.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.is_mandatory_keyword"></a>

#### is\_mandatory\_keyword

```python
def is_mandatory_keyword(keyword: str) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``keyword`` is a MANDATORY keyword (MUST / MUST NOT /
REQUIRED / SHALL / SHALL NOT): an absolute requirement whose violation is
non-conformance. (§2, R-29.1-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.is_advisory_keyword"></a>

#### is\_advisory\_keyword

```python
def is_advisory_keyword(keyword: str) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``keyword`` is ADVISORY (SHOULD / SHOULD NOT / RECOMMENDED):
valid reasons may exist to deviate, but the full implications must be understood
and weighed. (§2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.is_optional_keyword"></a>

#### is\_optional\_keyword

```python
def is_optional_keyword(keyword: str) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``keyword`` is OPTIONAL (MAY / OPTIONAL): truly
discretionary; an implementation that omits the behavior remains conformant.
(§2, §29.5)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement"></a>

## ConformanceRequirement Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ConformanceRequirement()
```

One normative requirement ("atom") of §29/§30, identified by its stable
requirement id, the section it belongs to, the role(s)/axis it binds, and its
RFC 2119 level. This is the data form of the story's §7 behavior table; a
conformance harness enumerates it to know exactly what to check.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.id"></a>

#### id

The stable requirement id, e.g. ``"R-29.2-h"``. (story §10 traceability)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.section"></a>

#### section

The §29/§30 subsection this atom belongs to, e.g. ``"29.2"``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.keyword"></a>

#### keyword

The RFC 2119 keyword exactly as the story marks it. (§2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.level"></a>

#### level

The canonical level family derived from :attr:`keyword`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.axis"></a>

#### axis

Which conformance axis the requirement constrains. (§29.1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.roles"></a>

#### roles

The role(s) the requirement binds; empty ⇒ binds every role. (§29.1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceRequirement.statement"></a>

#### statement

A one-line restatement of the obligation.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CONFORMANCE_REQUIREMENTS"></a>

#### CONFORMANCE\_REQUIREMENTS

The complete registry of §29/§30 normative requirements, in document order.
(§29.1–§29.9, §30) Each entry mirrors exactly one ``[R-… · KEYWORD]`` atom from
the story's §7; the keyword and level honor the spec verbatim. A conformance
suite iterates this to enumerate every applicable obligation for a profile
(see :func:`requirements_for_profile`).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.lookup_requirement"></a>

#### lookup\_requirement

```python
def lookup_requirement(id: str) -> ConformanceRequirement | None
```

Look up a requirement by its id (e.g. ``"R-29.2-h"``), or ``None``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.requirements_for_axis"></a>

#### requirements\_for\_axis

```python
def requirements_for_axis(
        axis: ConformanceAxis) -> list[ConformanceRequirement]
```

Return every requirement whose ``axis`` matches. (§29.1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.requirements_for_role"></a>

#### requirements\_for\_role

```python
def requirements_for_role(
        role: ConformanceRole) -> list[ConformanceRequirement]
```

Return every requirement that binds ``role``. A requirement with an empty
``roles`` list binds every role; otherwise it binds only the named roles.
(§29.1 item 1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CapabilityObligation"></a>

## CapabilityObligation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CapabilityObligation()
```

One capability-conditioned obligation: advertising ``capability`` binds the
advertising ``party`` to the MUST-level requirements of ``section``. (§29.4 item 1,
R-29.4-b – R-29.4-g) The data form of "advertise implies implement".

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CapabilityObligation.capability"></a>

#### capability

The advertised capability identifier (Appendix D / §6).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CapabilityObligation.party"></a>

#### party

Which party advertises and is thereby bound.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CapabilityObligation.section"></a>

#### section

The spec section whose MUST-level behavior the advertiser must satisfy.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CapabilityObligation.additional_sections"></a>

#### additional\_sections

Any additional sections also bound by this capability (e.g. subscriptions → §10).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CAPABILITY_OBLIGATIONS"></a>

#### CAPABILITY\_OBLIGATIONS

The per-capability obligation map of §29.4: each advertised capability binds its
advertiser to a feature section's MUST-level behavior. (R-29.4-b – R-29.4-g)

  tools        → §16
  resources    → §17  (resources.subscribe additionally → §10)
  prompts      → §18
  completions  → §19
  elicitation  → §20  (client)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.obligation_for_capability"></a>

#### obligation\_for\_capability

```python
def obligation_for_capability(capability: str) -> CapabilityObligation | None
```

Return the obligation a party incurs by advertising ``capability``, or ``None``
when the capability carries no enumerated feature-section obligation beyond the
baseline. (§29.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.obliged_sections_for_capabilities"></a>

#### obliged\_sections\_for\_capabilities

```python
def obliged_sections_for_capabilities(advertised: Iterable[str]) -> list[str]
```

Return the spec sections whose MUST-level behavior an implementation is bound
to, given the capabilities it advertises. (§29.4 item 1, R-29.4-a – R-29.4-g)

The result is deterministic, de-duplicated, and includes the additional sections
(e.g. ``resources.subscribe`` adds ``"10"``), sorted numerically.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ServerRequestDisposition"></a>

## ServerRequestDisposition Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ServerRequestDisposition()
```

The disposition a conformant server reaches for an incoming request after the
ordered §29.2 checks. Either a rejection carrying the registry-exact code, or
acceptance (the request proceeds to a resultType-tagged success). (§29.2)

``ok`` is the discriminator. On rejection, ``stage`` names which §29.2 check
failed:

  - ``"revision"``   — §29.2 item 4: unsupported declared revision. (R-29.2-h)
    carries ``code`` (-32004) and ``data = {"supported": [...], "requested": ...}``;
  - ``"envelope"``   — §29.2 item 6: a §4-required field is missing/malformed.
    (R-29.2-j) carries ``code`` (-32602) and ``message``;
  - ``"capability"`` — §29.2 item 5: a required client capability not declared.
    (R-29.2-i, R-29.4-k) carries ``code`` (-32003) and
    ``data = {"requiredCapabilities": {...}}``;
  - ``"gating"``     — §29.2 item 8: the feature is not gated by an advertised
    capability. (R-29.2-m, R-29.2-n) carries ``reason = "not-advertised"``.

On acceptance ``ok`` is ``True`` and every other field is ``None``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ServerRequestContext"></a>

## ServerRequestContext Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ServerRequestContext()
```

Inputs to :func:`classify_server_request`: a single self-contained §4 request
and the server's surface.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ServerRequestContext.meta"></a>

#### meta

The request's ``params._meta`` envelope (raw).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ServerRequestContext.server_supported_revisions"></a>

#### server\_supported\_revisions

The revisions the server supports (always includes the wire value).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ServerRequestContext.required_client_capabilities"></a>

#### required\_client\_capabilities

The capabilities required to process this request, as a ClientCapabilities map.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ServerRequestContext.feature_advertised"></a>

#### feature\_advertised

Whether the requested feature is gated behind a capability the server advertised.
``None`` means "not applicable / no gate to evaluate"; ``False`` means refused.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.classify_server_request"></a>

#### classify\_server\_request

```python
def classify_server_request(
        ctx: ServerRequestContext) -> ServerRequestDisposition
```

Apply the ordered §29.2 baseline-server request checks to ONE self-contained §4
request and return its :class:`ServerRequestDisposition`. (§29.2, R-29.2-e –
R-29.2-n, R-29.4-k)

The checks run strictly in the §7 flow order — judged on this request's own
envelope, NEVER on connection or prior-request state (R-29.1-e, R-29.2-f):

  1. revision supported?                  → else -32004 (data: supported, requested)
  2. all §4-required fields present?      → else -32602 (Invalid params)
  3. required client capability declared? → else -32003 (data.requiredCapabilities)
  4. feature gated by advertised cap?     → else refuse (not advertised)
  else → accept (proceeds to a resultType-tagged success).

Reuses :func:`validate_request_meta` for the envelope check (so the same
required-field set is honored) and :func:`compute_missing_client_capabilities` for
the capability gate. The revision check uses the declared revision from the
envelope against ``server_supported_revisions``.

Note the ordering rationale: a malformed protocol-version field (not a
well-formed-but-unsupported revision) is an envelope failure (-32602), so the
revision check first asks whether the declared revision is a well-formed,
server-unsupported one; a structurally invalid envelope falls through to the
-32602 stage.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.SuccessResultTypeValidation"></a>

## SuccessResultTypeValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class SuccessResultTypeValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_success_result_type`.

``ok=True`` carries the recognized ``result_type``. ``ok=False`` carries a
``reason`` — ``"missing"`` (no discriminator) or ``"not-advertised"`` (present but
not in the accepted set), and ``result_type`` only for the latter.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.validate_success_result_type"></a>

#### validate\_success\_result\_type

```python
def validate_success_result_type(
    result: dict,
    active_extension_set: Iterable[str] = (),
    extension_result_types: Mapping[str, Iterable[str]] | None = None
) -> SuccessResultTypeValidation
```

Assert that a successful result carries a ``resultType`` discriminator drawn from

the core set plus the values of advertised extensions only. (§29.2 items 7 & 8,
R-29.2-k, R-29.2-l)

Returns ``ok=False`` with ``reason`` when the discriminator is absent
(``"missing"``) or present but not in the accepted set (``"not-advertised"``).
Reuses :func:`is_result_type_accepted` (S38) so the accepted set is exactly the
core values plus those contributed by extensions in ``active_extension_set``.

**Arguments**:

- `result`: The success result object (raw).
- `active_extension_set`: The extensions active for this interaction.
- `extension_result_types`: Map of extension id → the resultType values it
contributes.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.client_request_carries_baseline_envelope"></a>

#### client\_request\_carries\_baseline\_envelope

```python
def client_request_carries_baseline_envelope(meta: dict) -> bool
```

Validate that a client request's metadata carries the three §4-required fields —
protocol revision, client identity, and client capabilities — that baseline client
conformance mandates on EVERY request. (§29.3 item 1, R-29.3-a)

A thin, intention-revealing wrapper over :func:`validate_request_meta` so the
client-side baseline check and the server-side envelope check share one
required-field definition (the stateless model forbids relying on a remembered
earlier request).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.REQUIRED_CLIENT_REQUEST_META_KEYS"></a>

#### REQUIRED\_CLIENT\_REQUEST\_META\_KEYS

The fields a client MUST include in every request's per-request metadata. (§29.3
item 1, R-29.3-a) Exposed for a conformance harness to assert presence.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.InputRequiredRetryValidation"></a>

## InputRequiredRetryValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class InputRequiredRetryValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_input_required_retry`.

``ok=False`` carries the first violated rule in ``reason``:
  - ``"reused-id"``       — the retry reused the original request id;
  - ``"state-mismatch"``  — provided requestState was not echoed byte-for-byte;
  - ``"unexpected-state"``— a requestState was included when none was provided.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.validate_input_required_retry"></a>

#### validate\_input\_required\_retry

```python
def validate_input_required_retry(
        *,
        original_id: str | int,
        retry_id: str | int,
        provided_state: object = _NO_STATE,
        retry_state: object = _NO_STATE) -> InputRequiredRetryValidation
```

Validate a client's retry request after an ``input_required`` result. (§29.3

item 4, R-29.3-j)

The retry MUST: (a) use a request id distinct from the original, (b) echo
``requestState`` byte-for-byte when one was provided, and (c) omit ``requestState``
when none was provided.

Returns ``ok=False`` with ``reason`` identifying the first violated rule, else
``ok=True``. ``requestState`` comparison is strict equality (the value is opaque
and echoed exactly, R-29.3-f). Omit ``provided_state`` / ``retry_state`` (or leave
them at the absent sentinel) to model "no state".

**Arguments**:

- `original_id`: The original request's id.
- `retry_id`: The retry request's id (must differ).
- `provided_state`: The requestState the server provided, or absent when none.
- `retry_state`: The requestState the retry carries, or absent when none.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.INPUT_REQUEST_REQUIRED_CAPABILITY"></a>

#### INPUT\_REQUEST\_REQUIRED\_CAPABILITY

The map from an input-request kind to the client capability that authorizes a
server to place it into an ``input_required`` result. (§29.4 item 5, R-29.4-l)
A server MUST NOT include an input request of a kind the client has not declared
(e.g. no elicitation input request without the elicitation capability).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.may_place_input_request"></a>

#### may\_place\_input\_request

```python
def may_place_input_request(method: str, client_capabilities: dict) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when a server MAY place an input request of ``method`` into an
``input_required`` result for a client declaring ``client_capabilities``. (§29.4
item 5, R-29.4-l)

An unrecognized method is rejected (``False``): a server must not solicit a kind it
cannot tie to a declared capability. Reuses :data:`RECOGNIZED_INPUT_REQUEST_METHODS`
(S17) for the recognized-kind set and :data:`INPUT_REQUEST_REQUIRED_CAPABILITY` for
the gating capability.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.RobustnessDisposition"></a>

#### RobustnessDisposition

How a conformant receiver disposes of an element of a received message under the
§29.6 robustness rules. (§29.6)

  - ``accept``         — a recognized, understood element: process it normally;
  - ``ignore``         — an unrecognized field/capability/extension: ignore it,
                         do NOT reject the message (R-29.6-b/c/d);
  - ``treat-as-error`` — an unrecognized resultType: the whole response is an
                         error and MUST NOT be acted upon (R-29.6-f/g);
  - ``fail-request``   — an unrecognized error code: a request failure surfaced
                         via message/data, never a crash/misclassification (R-29.6-e).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.RobustnessElement"></a>

#### RobustnessElement

The kind of received element being disposed of under §29.6.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.robustness_disposition"></a>

#### robustness\_disposition

```python
def robustness_disposition(element: RobustnessElement,
                           recognized: bool) -> RobustnessDisposition
```

Compute the §29.6 robustness disposition for one received element, given whether
the receiver recognizes it. (§29.6, R-29.6-a – R-29.6-h)

  - an unknown ``field``/``capability``/``extension`` → ``ignore`` (never reject);
  - an unknown ``result-type`` → ``treat-as-error`` (must not act on it);
  - an unknown ``error-code``  → ``fail-request`` (surface as a failure);
  - any recognized element      → ``accept``.

This NEVER discards understood content: robustness applies only to the unrecognized
(R-29.6-i) — a recognized element always returns ``accept``. The absence of a
resultType is handled by :func:`interpret_result_type` (the §3 absence rule,
R-29.6-h), not here.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ResultActionDecision"></a>

## ResultActionDecision Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ResultActionDecision()
```

Outcome of :func:`decide_result_action`.

``act=True`` carries the recognized ``result_type`` (the receiver MAY act).
``act=False`` carries ``reason="unrecognized"`` and the offending ``result_type``
(treat the whole response as an error, do not act).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.decide_result_action"></a>

#### decide\_result\_action

```python
def decide_result_action(
    result: dict,
    active_extension_set: Iterable[str] = (),
    extension_result_types: Mapping[str, Iterable[str]] | None = None
) -> ResultActionDecision
```

Apply the §29.6 + §3 receiver rules to a result's ``resultType``. (R-29.6-f,
R-29.6-g, R-29.6-h) Returns:

- ``act=True`` with ``result_type``   — recognized (core or, when supplied, an
accepted extension value): the receiver MAY act on the result;
- ``act=False`` with ``reason="unrecognized"`` and ``result_type`` — present but
not accepted: treat the whole response as an error, do not act (R-29.6-f/g).

An ABSENT discriminator is resolved by the §3 absence rule via
:func:`interpret_result_type` (treated as ``"complete"``, recognized) so the
receiver acts on it (R-29.6-h).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.STATELESS_CONFORMANCE_INVARIANTS"></a>

#### STATELESS\_CONFORMANCE\_INVARIANTS

The stateless-model invariants that bind every role. (§29.7, R-29.7-a – R-29.7-e)
A flat, enumerable restatement a conformance harness can assert against.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.RequestStateHandling"></a>

## RequestStateHandling Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RequestStateHandling()
```

Outcome of :func:`decide_request_state_handling`.

``trust`` is always ``"untrusted"``; ``action`` is ``"reject"`` only for a
security-significant value whose integrity check failed, else ``"accept"``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.decide_request_state_handling"></a>

#### decide\_request\_state\_handling

```python
def decide_request_state_handling(
        security_significant: bool,
        integrity_verified: bool) -> RequestStateHandling
```

Decide how a server must treat a ``requestState`` value that passed through a

client. (§29.7 item 4, R-29.7-d, R-29.7-e)

It is ALWAYS attacker-controlled input; when it influences authorization, resource
access, or business logic the server MUST verify its integrity and reject what
fails.

**Arguments**:

- `security_significant`: Whether the value influences authz/resource/business logic.
- `integrity_verified`: Whether the value's integrity check passed.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.STREAMABLE_HTTP_NEGOTIATION_ERROR_STATUS"></a>

#### STREAMABLE\_HTTP\_NEGOTIATION\_ERROR\_STATUS

The Streamable HTTP status a protocol error code maps to on that transport. (§29.8 item 3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.streamable_http_status_for_protocol_error"></a>

#### streamable\_http\_status\_for\_protocol\_error

```python
def streamable_http_status_for_protocol_error(code: int) -> int | None
```

Map a protocol error ``code`` to the HTTP status it MUST ride on the Streamable
HTTP transport for the §29.8 negotiation/envelope conditions. (§29.8 item 3,
R-29.8-c)

``-32602`` (malformed/missing field) and ``-32003`` (missing required client
capability) both map to ``400 Bad Request``; any other code returns ``None`` (its
mapping is governed by §9 / S34, not this conformance point).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.TransportConformance"></a>

## TransportConformance Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TransportConformance()
```

The conformance evaluation of a SINGLE transport an implementation offers. (§29.8)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.TransportConformance.transport"></a>

#### transport

The transport being evaluated.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.TransportConformance.authorization_applies"></a>

#### authorization\_applies

Whether the authorization framework SHOULD apply (HTTP) — R-29.8-d.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.TransportConformance.authorization_forbidden"></a>

#### authorization\_forbidden

Whether the authorization framework SHOULD NOT apply (stdio) — R-29.8-e.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.TransportConformance.credential_conveyance"></a>

#### credential\_conveyance

How credentials are conveyed for this transport.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.evaluate_transport_conformance"></a>

#### evaluate\_transport\_conformance

```python
def evaluate_transport_conformance(
        transport: ConformanceTransport) -> TransportConformance
```

Evaluate the authorization-applicability conformance points for a single
transport. (§29.8 items 4 & 5, R-29.8-d, R-29.8-e)

Mirrors S35's HTTP-vs-stdio rule with one source of truth: an HTTP-based transport
SHOULD conform to authorization; a stdio transport SHOULD NOT apply it and obtains
credentials from its environment.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfile"></a>

## ConformanceProfile Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ConformanceProfile()
```

The abstract descriptor that fully describes an implementation's conformance: the
tuple of roles, advertised revisions, advertised capabilities, advertised
extensions, and implemented transports. (§29.9 item 3, story §6) NOT a wire message
— it is used to reason about and report conformance.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfile.roles"></a>

#### roles

The role(s) the implementation plays; binds it to each role's requirements. (R-29.1-b)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfile.revisions"></a>

#### revisions

The advertised protocol revisions; MUST include the wire value ``2026-07-28``. (R-29.9-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfile.capabilities"></a>

#### capabilities

The advertised capability identifiers (Appendix D / §6).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfile.extensions"></a>

#### extensions

The advertised extension identifiers; MAY be empty (zero extensions is conformant). (R-29.5-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfile.transports"></a>

#### transports

The implemented transports; at least one, each independently conformant. (R-29.8-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfileViolation"></a>

## ConformanceProfileViolation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ConformanceProfileViolation()
```

A single way a :class:`ConformanceProfile` fails to be well-formed.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfileViolation.field"></a>

#### field

Which profile field the violation concerns.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfileViolation.message"></a>

#### message

Human-readable description of the violation, citing the requirement.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.ConformanceProfileValidation"></a>

## ConformanceProfileValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ConformanceProfileValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_conformance_profile`.

``ok=True`` ⇒ no violations. ``ok=False`` ⇒ ``violations`` accumulates EVERY
well-formedness failure.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.validate_conformance_profile"></a>

#### validate\_conformance\_profile

```python
def validate_conformance_profile(
        profile: ConformanceProfile) -> ConformanceProfileValidation
```

Validate that a :class:`ConformanceProfile` is well-formed against the structural
requirements of §29. (§29.5 item 2, §29.8 item 1, §29.9 item 3, R-29.1-b, R-29.5-c,
R-29.8-a, R-29.9-c) Accumulates ALL violations:

  - ``roles``      — at least one, each a recognized role (R-29.1-a/b);
  - ``revisions``  — non-empty and MUST include ``2026-07-28`` (R-29.9-c);
  - ``extensions`` — every identifier well-formed per §6 naming (R-29.5-c); an empty
    list is fully conformant (R-29.5-a);
  - ``transports`` — at least one transport (R-29.8-a).

``capabilities`` are not constrained here beyond being a list — an unrecognized
capability is tolerated by robustness (R-29.6-c), not a profile error.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.profile_supports_revision"></a>

#### profile\_supports\_revision

```python
def profile_supports_revision(profile: ConformanceProfile,
                              revision: str) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``revision`` is supported as a profile revision: it is the
current wire value, or any revision the profile advertises. (§29.9 item 3)

Reuses :func:`is_supported_protocol_version` for the baseline wire value.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.requirements_for_profile"></a>

#### requirements\_for\_profile

```python
def requirements_for_profile(
        profile: ConformanceProfile) -> list[ConformanceRequirement]
```

Enumerate every normative requirement that APPLIES to a profile: every baseline
requirement for the role(s) it plays, plus every transport requirement (an
implementation always implements at least one transport). (§29.1, §29.9 item 1)

The result is the exact obligation set a conformance harness must verify for this
implementation — no more, no less.

Feature-axis requirements that are unconditional (the baseline §29.1, §29.6, §29.7,
§29.9 atoms) always apply; the capability-conditioned §29.4 atoms apply only when
the relevant capability is advertised — callers combine this with
:func:`obliged_sections_for_capabilities` for the feature-section MUST-level
behaviors owned by other stories.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.satisfies_role"></a>

#### satisfies\_role

```python
def satisfies_role(satisfied_roles: Iterable[ConformanceRole],
                   target_role: ConformanceRole) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when an implementation satisfying ONLY one role's requirements is

conformant for ``target_role``. (§29.1, R-29.1-a, R-29.1-b)

A both-roles implementation must satisfy each role; satisfying only the other
role's requirements is non-conformant for ``target_role``.

**Arguments**:

- `satisfied_roles`: The roles whose requirements the implementation provably satisfies.
- `target_role`: The role whose conformance is being judged.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.FeatureConformance"></a>

## FeatureConformance Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class FeatureConformance()
```

Outcome of :func:`is_feature_fully_conformant`.

``ok=False`` carries ``reason="advertised-not-implemented"`` — the non-conformant
intermediate state in which a feature is advertised but only partially implemented.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.is_feature_fully_conformant"></a>

#### is\_feature\_fully\_conformant

```python
def is_feature_fully_conformant(advertised: bool,
                                fully_implemented: bool) -> FeatureConformance
```

Enforce "no partial feature conformance": an implementation either fully satisfies

the MUST-level behavior of an advertised feature or MUST NOT advertise it. (§29.9
item 4, R-29.9-b; the §29.4 advertise-implies-implement rule, R-29.4-a, R-29.4-j)

Returns ``ok=False`` with ``reason="advertised-not-implemented"`` when a feature is
advertised but not fully implemented (the non-conformant intermediate state), and
``ok=True`` otherwise — including when an UNadvertised feature is not implemented
(perfectly conformant) and when an advertised feature IS fully implemented.

**Arguments**:

- `advertised`: Whether the feature is advertised.
- `fully_implemented`: Whether every MUST-level behavior of the feature is implemented.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.CITATION_STATUS"></a>

#### CITATION\_STATUS

The status the §30 citation markers carry: provenance only, never load-bearing.
(§30, R-30-a) No normative behavior, code, name, or wire format depends on the
content of any citation; stripping or altering a marker changes nothing observable.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.conformance_requirements.is_citation_load_bearing"></a>

#### is\_citation\_load\_bearing

```python
def is_citation_load_bearing(_citation_marker: str) -> bool
```

Return ``False`` always: no §30 citation marker is ever load-bearing. (R-30-a)

Provided as a predicate so a conformance harness can assert that removing a citation
changes no required behavior — the answer is unconditionally "not load-bearing",
independent of which marker is named.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery"></a>

# stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery

Discovery via ``server/discover`` (§5.3).

Discovery is the one well-known entry point a client uses to learn what a server is and
can do before issuing feature requests: a single round trip in which the client sends
``server/discover`` and the server returns a ``DiscoverResult`` advertising its supported
protocol revisions, capabilities, and identity — or, when it does not support the
requested revision, an ``UnsupportedProtocolVersion`` (-32004) error whose
``data.supported`` still tells the client which revisions the server accepts.

This module provides the request/result validators, the reference handler every server
MUST implement (R-5.3-a), request/response builders, and order-independent client-side
revision selection (R-5.3.2-d). Wire shapes are represented as plain dicts.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.SERVER_DISCOVER_METHOD"></a>

#### SERVER\_DISCOVER\_METHOD

The discovery method name. (Owned by the progress module in the TS SDK; pinned here
as the literal to avoid a forward dependency.)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverConfig"></a>

## DiscoverConfig Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class DiscoverConfig()
```

The server-supplied inputs to a ``DiscoverResult``.

Constructed once and reused across discovery requests (the model is stateless — the
result does not depend on the connection or any prior request).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverConfig.supported_versions"></a>

#### supported\_versions

Non-empty list of revisions the server will accept. (R-5.3.2-b, R-5.3.2-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverConfig.capabilities"></a>

#### capabilities

The server's advertised capabilities; ``{}`` means "no optional capabilities".

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverConfig.server_info"></a>

#### server\_info

Server identity; MUST carry string ``name`` and ``version``. (R-5.3.2-f)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverConfig.instructions"></a>

#### instructions

OPTIONAL guidance for using the server effectively. (R-5.3.2-g)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverConfig.meta"></a>

#### meta

OPTIONAL result-level metadata. (R-5.3.2-k)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.is_version_supported"></a>

#### is\_version\_supported

```python
def is_version_supported(supported_versions: list[str],
                         requested: str) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``requested`` is one of ``supported_versions``.

Exact string membership (no lexical/chronological ordering, §5.1) and independent of
element order — reordering ``supported_versions`` never changes the outcome.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult"></a>

## DiscoverResult Objects

```python
class DiscoverResult(McpModel)
```

A successful ``server/discover`` result (§5.3.2) — the Python analogue of the TS
``DiscoverResultSchema``.

Advertises the server's supported protocol revisions, capabilities, and identity.
Unknown members pass through (forward-compatible).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult.result_type"></a>

#### result\_type

REQUIRED base discriminator (a tools/discover result is ``"complete"``). (R-5.3.2-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult.supported_versions"></a>

#### supported\_versions

REQUIRED non-empty list of revisions the server accepts. (R-5.3.2-b, R-5.3.2-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult.capabilities"></a>

#### capabilities

REQUIRED advertised capabilities; ``{}`` ⇒ no optional capabilities.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult.server_info"></a>

#### server\_info

REQUIRED server identity. (R-5.3.2-f)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult.instructions"></a>

#### instructions

OPTIONAL guidance for using the server effectively. (R-5.3.2-g)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverResult.meta"></a>

#### meta

OPTIONAL result-level metadata. (R-5.3.2-k)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.is_discover_result"></a>

#### is\_discover\_result

```python
def is_discover_result(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``value`` is a well-formed ``DiscoverResult`` (§5.3.2).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.build_discover_result"></a>

#### build\_discover\_result

```python
def build_discover_result(config: DiscoverConfig) -> dict
```

Build a successful ``DiscoverResult`` from a server's :class:`DiscoverConfig`.

``resultType`` is ``"complete"`` (R-5.3.2-a); optional ``instructions`` / ``_meta`` are
included only when supplied — never defaulted.

**Raises**:

- `ValueError`: when ``supported_versions`` is empty — a server MUST advertise at
least one accepted revision (R-5.3.2-b). (Python analogue of JS ``RangeError``.)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.build_unsupported_protocol_version_error"></a>

#### build\_unsupported\_protocol\_version\_error

```python
def build_unsupported_protocol_version_error(requested: str,
                                             supported: list[str]) -> dict
```

Build the ``UnsupportedProtocolVersion`` (-32004) error.

Both ``data.supported`` and ``data.requested`` are REQUIRED (§5.5): the former still
advertises the server's revisions so the client can recover; the latter echoes the
rejected revision. (R-5.3.1-g)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.DiscoverRequestValidation"></a>

## DiscoverRequestValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class DiscoverRequestValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_discover_request`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.validate_discover_request"></a>

#### validate\_discover\_request

```python
def validate_discover_request(request: object) -> DiscoverRequestValidation
```

Validate a raw ``server/discover`` request payload (§5.3.1).

Checks, in order: the object is present with ``method == "server/discover"``; ``params``
is an object carrying ``_meta``; ``_meta`` carries the three REQUIRED reserved keys with
correct types (delegated to :func:`validate_request_meta`). Extra ``_meta`` keys are
accepted (R-5.3.1-e). On success returns the declared revision.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.ProcessDiscoverOutcome"></a>

## ProcessDiscoverOutcome Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ProcessDiscoverOutcome()
```

Outcome of :func:`process_discover_request`.

``ok=True`` carries ``result`` (a ``DiscoverResult`` dict); otherwise ``error`` carries
either an invalid-params (-32602) or an UnsupportedProtocolVersion (-32004) error dict.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.process_discover_request"></a>

#### process\_discover\_request

```python
def process_discover_request(config: DiscoverConfig,
                             request: object) -> ProcessDiscoverOutcome
```

The reference ``server/discover`` handler every server MUST implement (R-5.3-a).

* A malformed request → invalid-params (-32602).
* A well-formed request whose declared revision the server does NOT support → an
  ``UnsupportedProtocolVersion`` (-32004) error (never a crash/hang); ``data.supported``
  lists the server's revisions, ``data.requested`` echoes the rejected one. (R-5.3.1-f/-g)
* Otherwise → a ``DiscoverResult``.

Stateless: the requested revision is derived solely from the request's ``_meta``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.build_discover_request"></a>

#### build\_discover\_request

```python
def build_discover_request(id_: RequestId,
                           protocol_version: str,
                           client_info: dict,
                           client_capabilities: dict,
                           extra_meta: dict | None = None) -> dict
```

Build a complete ``server/discover`` JSON-RPC request carrying the three REQUIRED
reserved ``_meta`` keys, plus any additional ``_meta`` keys (§5.3.1, R-5.3.1-e).

Reserved keys always win over ``extra_meta``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.build_discover_response"></a>

#### build\_discover\_response

```python
def build_discover_response(id_: RequestId, config: DiscoverConfig) -> dict
```

Wrap a ``DiscoverResult`` in its JSON-RPC success envelope (§5.3.2).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.select_revision"></a>

#### select\_revision

```python
def select_revision(supported_versions: list[str],
                    client_acceptable: list[str] | None = None) -> str | None
```

Select a revision from the server's ``supported_versions`` using the client's own
preference order — never the order of the server's list (R-5.3.2-d, AC-08.7).

``client_acceptable`` is the client's revisions in descending preference (defaults to
``[CURRENT_PROTOCOL_VERSION]``). Returns the first client-preferred revision the server
also supports, or ``None`` when they share none. Reordering ``supported_versions``
cannot change the result.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.discovery.resolve_instructions"></a>

#### resolve\_instructions

```python
def resolve_instructions(result: dict) -> str | None
```

Return the server's ``instructions`` string, or ``None`` when absent.

When ``instructions`` is missing the client MUST NOT assume or fabricate guidance —
this returns ``None`` rather than an empty/default string. (R-5.3.2-j, AC-08.11)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation"></a>

# stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation

Elicitation I — capability, delivery & modes (§20.1–§20.3).

A server's request for structured user input, gathered and returned through the client.
NOT a server-initiated request: the server returns an ``input_required`` result carrying an
``elicitation/create`` request (the §11 multi-round-trip mechanism). This port owns the
front half: the ``elicitation`` capability (``form``/``url`` sub-flags) + gating, the
embedded ``ElicitRequest`` and its two modes (form ``requestedSchema``; url navigable URL),
mode resolution, the flat-``requestedSchema`` validator, and the builders.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_elicitation_mode"></a>

#### is\_elicitation\_mode

```python
def is_elicitation_mode(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``value`` is one of the two defined modes. (§20.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.ElicitationCapabilityValue"></a>

## ElicitationCapabilityValue Objects

```python
class ElicitationCapabilityValue(McpModel)
```

The ``elicitation`` capability value (§20.1) — an object with OPTIONAL object
``form`` / ``url`` sub-flags; extra members pass through. (R-20.1-f)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_elicitation_capability_value"></a>

#### is\_valid\_elicitation\_capability\_value

```python
def is_valid_elicitation_capability_value(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a valid ``elicitation`` capability value: an object with OPTIONAL
object ``form``/``url`` sub-flags. (§20.1, R-20.1-f)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.RequestedSchema"></a>

## RequestedSchema Objects

```python
class RequestedSchema(McpModel)
```

A form-mode ``requestedSchema`` (§20.3): ``type == "object"`` + a ``properties`` map of
object schemas; OPTIONAL ``required`` (list of strings) and ``$schema`` (string). Other
JSON-Schema keywords pass through. Flatness (each property primitive) is enforced
separately by :func:`validate_requested_schema`. (R-20.3-e/-f/-g/-h)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_requested_schema"></a>

#### is\_valid\_requested\_schema

```python
def is_valid_requested_schema(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a structurally valid form ``requestedSchema``: ``type == "object"``,
``properties`` a map of objects, OPTIONAL ``required`` list of strings, OPTIONAL ``$schema``
string. (§20.3, R-20.3-e/-f/-g/-h) Flatness is checked by :func:`validate_requested_schema`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.RequestedSchemaValidation"></a>

## RequestedSchemaValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class RequestedSchemaValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_requested_schema`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.validate_requested_schema"></a>

#### validate\_requested\_schema

```python
def validate_requested_schema(value: object) -> RequestedSchemaValidation
```

Validate the structural restrictions on a form ``requestedSchema``: ``type`` ``"object"``,
a FLAT ``properties`` map (each property primitive — no nesting keywords, no
object/array type), and every ``required`` entry declared. (§20.3/§20.4, R-20.3-e/-f/-g)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_elicitation_url"></a>

#### is\_valid\_elicitation\_url

```python
def is_valid_elicitation_url(url: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``url`` is a valid absolute URI/URL (a scheme + authority or path).
(§20.3, R-20.3-m/-n)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.FormElicitParams"></a>

## FormElicitParams Objects

```python
class FormElicitParams(McpModel)
```

Form-mode ``ElicitRequest`` params (§20.3): OPTIONAL ``mode == "form"``, REQUIRED string
``message``, REQUIRED ``requestedSchema`` (a flat-object schema).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.UrlElicitParams"></a>

## UrlElicitParams Objects

```python
class UrlElicitParams(McpModel)
```

URL-mode ``ElicitRequest`` params (§20.3): REQUIRED ``mode == "url"``, string ``message``,
non-empty ``elicitationId``, and a valid ``url``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_form_params"></a>

#### is\_valid\_form\_params

```python
def is_valid_form_params(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for form-mode params: OPTIONAL ``mode == "form"``, REQUIRED string
``message``, REQUIRED ``requestedSchema``. (§20.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_url_params"></a>

#### is\_valid\_url\_params

```python
def is_valid_url_params(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for url-mode params: REQUIRED ``mode == "url"``, string ``message``,
non-empty ``elicitationId``, valid ``url``. (§20.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_elicit_request_params"></a>

#### is\_valid\_elicit\_request\_params

```python
def is_valid_elicit_request_params(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for the mode union (url tried first; form accepts absent ``mode``). (§20.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_valid_elicit_request"></a>

#### is\_valid\_elicit\_request

```python
def is_valid_elicit_request(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a well-formed ``ElicitRequest``: ``method == "elicitation/create"`` +
valid mode-specific ``params``. (§20.2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.is_elicitation_create_request"></a>

#### is\_elicitation\_create\_request

```python
def is_elicitation_create_request(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for the exact ``"elicitation/create"`` method literal (method-only check).
(§20.2, R-20.2-b)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.resolve_elicitation_mode"></a>

#### resolve\_elicitation\_mode

```python
def resolve_elicitation_mode(params: object) -> str | None
```

Resolve the effective mode: absent/``"form"`` → ``"form"`` (implicit baseline), ``"url"``
→ ``"url"``, anything else → ``None``. (§20.3, R-20.3-b/-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.client_supports_elicitation"></a>

#### client\_supports\_elicitation

```python
def client_supports_elicitation(client_caps: dict) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when the client declares ``elicitation``. (§20.1, R-20.1-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.supported_elicitation_modes"></a>

#### supported\_elicitation\_modes

```python
def supported_elicitation_modes(client_caps: dict) -> list[str]
```

Return the modes the client supports: declaring ``elicitation`` always implies ``form``
(baseline); ``url`` only with the ``elicitation.url`` sub-flag; ``[]`` when undeclared.
(§20.1, R-20.1-c/-f)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.client_supports_elicitation_mode"></a>

#### client\_supports\_elicitation\_mode

```python
def client_supports_elicitation_mode(client_caps: dict, mode: str) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when the client supports ``mode``. (§20.1, R-20.1-c/-f)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.ElicitationGateResult"></a>

## ElicitationGateResult Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ElicitationGateResult()
```

Outcome of :func:`gate_elicitation_request`: ``ok`` or a ``rejection`` dict.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.gate_elicitation_request"></a>

#### gate\_elicitation\_request

```python
def gate_elicitation_request(
        client_caps: dict,
        mode: str = ELICITATION_MODE_FORM) -> ElicitationGateResult
```

Decide whether a server MAY send an ``elicitation/create`` of ``mode``: rejected with
``capability-not-declared`` when ``elicitation`` is undeclared, or ``mode-not-supported``
when the mode isn't supported. (§20.1, R-20.1-d/-e)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.may_server_send_elicitation"></a>

#### may\_server\_send\_elicitation

```python
def may_server_send_elicitation(client_caps: dict,
                                mode: str = ELICITATION_MODE_FORM) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` exactly when :func:`gate_elicitation_request` permits the request. (§20.1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.build_form_elicit_request"></a>

#### build\_form\_elicit\_request

```python
def build_form_elicit_request(*,
                              message: str,
                              requested_schema: dict,
                              include_mode: bool = False) -> dict
```

Build a form-mode ``ElicitRequest`` (``mode`` omitted by default — the backwards-compatible

encoding). The schema is validated against the flat-object restriction first. (§20.2/§20.3)

**Raises**:

- `TypeError`: when ``requested_schema`` violates the restriction. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation.build_url_elicit_request"></a>

#### build\_url\_elicit\_request

```python
def build_url_elicit_request(*, message: str, elicitation_id: str,
                             url: str) -> dict
```

Build a url-mode ``ElicitRequest`` (``mode: "url"`` always emitted). (§20.2/§20.3)

**Raises**:

- `TypeError`: when ``url`` is invalid or ``elicitation_id`` is empty. (R-20.3-k/-n)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form"></a>

# stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation\_form

Elicitation II — restricted form schema, results & consent (§20.4–§20.8).

The payload + outcome surface for elicitation: the ``PrimitiveSchemaDefinition`` value type
(string/number/boolean + the five enum forms), the restricted form-schema validator, the
``content``↔``requestedSchema`` conformance check, the ``ElicitResult`` action semantics +
builders, the ``notifications/elicitation/complete`` notification, and the §20.7 consent /
anti-phishing helpers (sensitive-field detection, URL safety, consent presentation, user
identity binding). Builds on :mod:`stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_string_schema_format"></a>

#### is\_string\_schema\_format

```python
def is_string_schema_format(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for one of the four permitted ``StringSchema.format`` literals. (R-20.4-d)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.StringSchema"></a>

## StringSchema Objects

```python
class StringSchema(McpModel)
```

A free-text ``StringSchema`` primitive (§20.4): ``type: "string"`` + optional length
bounds / ``format`` / ``default``. Does not consider ``enum``/``oneOf`` (those select an
enum) — extra keys pass through.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.NumberSchema"></a>

## NumberSchema Objects

```python
class NumberSchema(McpModel)
```

A ``NumberSchema`` primitive (§20.4, R-20.4-e): ``type`` ``number``/``integer`` + optional
``minimum``/``maximum``/``default``.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.BooleanSchema"></a>

## BooleanSchema Objects

```python
class BooleanSchema(McpModel)
```

A ``BooleanSchema`` primitive (§20.4): ``type: "boolean"`` + optional ``default`` bool.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_valid_string_schema"></a>

#### is\_valid\_string\_schema

```python
def is_valid_string_schema(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a free-text ``StringSchema`` (``type: "string"``; optional length
bounds, ``format``, ``default``). Does not consider ``enum``/``oneOf``. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_valid_number_schema"></a>

#### is\_valid\_number\_schema

```python
def is_valid_number_schema(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a ``NumberSchema`` (``type`` ``number``/``integer``; optional bounds,
``default``). (§20.4, R-20.4-e)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_valid_boolean_schema"></a>

#### is\_valid\_boolean\_schema

```python
def is_valid_boolean_schema(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a ``BooleanSchema`` (``type: "boolean"``; optional ``default`` bool).
(§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.TitledEnumOption"></a>

## TitledEnumOption Objects

```python
class TitledEnumOption(McpModel)
```

One option of a titled enum: the wire ``const`` value plus its display ``title``.
Both are REQUIRED. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.UntitledSingleSelectEnum"></a>

## UntitledSingleSelectEnum Objects

```python
class UntitledSingleSelectEnum(McpModel)
```

A single choice from a list of string values, with no separate display labels:
``type: "string"`` + REQUIRED ``enum`` (``string[]``). (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.TitledSingleSelectEnum"></a>

## TitledSingleSelectEnum Objects

```python
class TitledSingleSelectEnum(McpModel)
```

A single choice where each option carries a display label via REQUIRED ``oneOf``
(one ``{const, title}`` entry per option). (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.UntitledMultiSelectItems"></a>

## UntitledMultiSelectItems Objects

```python
class UntitledMultiSelectItems(McpModel)
```

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.UntitledMultiSelectEnum"></a>

## UntitledMultiSelectEnum Objects

```python
class UntitledMultiSelectEnum(McpModel)
```

Selection of zero or more values from a list, no separate labels: ``type: "array"``
+ REQUIRED ``items`` (a string ``enum``) + optional count bounds. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.TitledMultiSelectItems"></a>

## TitledMultiSelectItems Objects

```python
class TitledMultiSelectItems(McpModel)
```

The ``items`` schema of a titled multi-select enum: an ``anyOf`` of options. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.TitledMultiSelectEnum"></a>

## TitledMultiSelectEnum Objects

```python
class TitledMultiSelectEnum(McpModel)
```

Selection of zero or more values where each option carries a display label via
REQUIRED ``items.anyOf`` + optional count bounds. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.LegacyTitledEnum"></a>

## LegacyTitledEnum Objects

```python
class LegacyTitledEnum(McpModel)
```

Deprecated legacy titled enum: per-value labels via a parallel ``enumNames`` array,
non-standard for JSON Schema 2020-12. Defined only for interoperability — a peer MAY
still send it; prefer :class:`TitledSingleSelectEnum` for new work. (§20.4, R-20.4-f, R-20.4-g)

.. deprecated::
  Use :class:`TitledSingleSelectEnum` for per-option labels in new functionality.
  (§20.4, R-20.4-f, R-20.4-g)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_valid_titled_enum_option"></a>

#### is\_valid\_titled\_enum\_option

```python
def is_valid_titled_enum_option(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a titled enum option: REQUIRED string ``const`` + ``title``. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.classify_enum_schema"></a>

#### classify\_enum\_schema

```python
def classify_enum_schema(value: object) -> str | None
```

Classify an enum schema into one of five structural forms by distinguishing keyword, or
``None`` when not an enum schema. ``enumNames`` marks the Deprecated legacy form. (§20.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_valid_enum_schema"></a>

#### is\_valid\_enum\_schema

```python
def is_valid_enum_schema(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for a member of the closed ``EnumSchema`` union — any of the five
structural forms. (§20.4)

Mirrors TS ``EnumSchemaSchema.safeParse``: validation is delegated to the
:data:`EnumSchema` union, while :func:`classify_enum_schema` reports the precise form.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.is_legacy_titled_enum_schema"></a>

#### is\_legacy\_titled\_enum\_schema

```python
def is_legacy_titled_enum_schema(value: object) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` for the Deprecated legacy enum form (string ``enum`` + ``enumNames``).
(§20.4, R-20.4-f)

.. deprecated::
  Use :class:`TitledSingleSelectEnum` for per-option labels in new functionality.
  (§20.4, R-20.4-f, R-20.4-g)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.elicitation_form.classify_primitive_schema"></a>

```python
def classify_primitive_schema(value: object) -> str | None
```

Classify a property schema by the ``PrimitiveSchemaDefinition`` member it selects
(``string``/``number``/``boolean``/``enum``), or ``None``. Structural per §20.4's table.

process-form-data | await-url-completion | declined | cancelled | malformed

```python
        mcp_session_subject: str | None,

```

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors"></a>

# stackific.mcp.protocol.errors

Error Handling & Error Code Registry (§22).

The authoritative model for how a receiver reports that a request could not be
processed: the JSON-RPC ``error`` object shape, the full registry of error codes (the
five standard JSON-RPC codes plus MCP's protocol-specific and transport codes), the
normative ``data`` payloads, the HTTP status mappings, the canonical mapping of
validation failures to ``-32602``, the firm boundary between a protocol-level
JSON-RPC ``error`` and a feature-level error *result* (a tool that ran and failed),
and the rules for extension-defined and unknown error codes.

Unlike the TypeScript SDK — where these numeric codes are first defined in their
owning feature modules and re-exported here — the Python port centralises every code
constant in this module (its canonical home). Later feature modules import the codes
they need from here, which keeps the dependency graph acyclic.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.PARSE_ERROR_CODE"></a>

#### PARSE\_ERROR\_CODE

Invalid JSON was received; the byte stream could not be parsed. (§22.2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.INVALID_REQUEST_CODE"></a>

#### INVALID\_REQUEST\_CODE

Valid JSON, but not a valid JSON-RPC request object. (§22.2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.METHOD_NOT_FOUND_CODE"></a>

#### METHOD\_NOT\_FOUND\_CODE

The method does not exist / is not available. (§22.2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.INVALID_PARAMS_CODE"></a>

#### INVALID\_PARAMS\_CODE

Invalid or malformed method parameters. (§22.2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.INTERNAL_ERROR_CODE"></a>

#### INTERNAL\_ERROR\_CODE

An unexpected condition prevented fulfilling a well-formed request. (§22.2)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.MISSING_CLIENT_CAPABILITY_CODE"></a>

#### MISSING\_CLIENT\_CAPABILITY\_CODE

The request requires a client capability the client did not declare. (§22.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.UNSUPPORTED_PROTOCOL_VERSION_CODE"></a>

#### UNSUPPORTED\_PROTOCOL\_VERSION\_CODE

The request's protocol revision is unknown to/unsupported by the server. (§22.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.HEADER_MISMATCH_CODE"></a>

#### HEADER\_MISMATCH\_CODE

A routing header is missing/malformed/mismatched (Streamable HTTP). (§22.7, §9.8)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.INVALID_CURSOR_CODE"></a>

#### INVALID\_CURSOR\_CODE

Invalid/expired cursor — an alias of invalid-params for bad cursors. (§12, §22.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND_LEGACY_CODE"></a>

#### RESOURCE\_NOT\_FOUND\_LEGACY\_CODE

Legacy "Resource not found" literal; §22.4 also maps this to -32602. (§22.4)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.JSONRPC_VERSION"></a>

#### JSONRPC\_VERSION

The JSON-RPC version marker every error response MUST carry. (R-22.1-d)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeClass"></a>

## ErrorCodeClass Objects

```python
class ErrorCodeClass(StrEnum)
```

The class a JSON-RPC error code falls into (§22). The numeric ``code`` is
authoritative; this taxonomy lets a receiver reason about a code it has never seen.
(R-22.1-h, R-22.7-a, R-22.7-e)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeClass.JSON_RPC_STANDARD"></a>

#### JSON\_RPC\_STANDARD

The five reserved JSON-RPC pre-defined codes (``-32700``, ``-32600..-32603``).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeClass.MCP_PROTOCOL"></a>

#### MCP\_PROTOCOL

MCP protocol-specific codes (``-32003``, ``-32004``) — normative ``data``. (§22.3)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeClass.SERVER_DEFINED"></a>

#### SERVER\_DEFINED

The implementation-defined server-error range ``-32000..-32099``. (§22.7)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeClass.EXTENSION_DEFINED"></a>

#### EXTENSION\_DEFINED

Any integer outside every reserved/server range — extension-defined. (§22.7)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.JSON_RPC_RESERVED_RANGE"></a>

#### JSON\_RPC\_RESERVED\_RANGE

JSON-RPC 2.0 reserved range for pre-defined errors, inclusive. (§22.2, §22.7)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.SERVER_ERROR_RANGE"></a>

#### SERVER\_ERROR\_RANGE

Implementation-defined server-error sub-range, inclusive. (§22.7) ``-32001`` lives here.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorDataPolicy"></a>

#### ErrorDataPolicy

Whether a code's ``data`` shape is normative (fixed by spec) or sender-defined.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry"></a>

## ErrorCodeRegistryEntry Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ErrorCodeRegistryEntry()
```

One row of the §22 error-code registry. (§6.5)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.code"></a>

#### code

The authoritative numeric code. (R-22.1-h)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.name"></a>

#### name

The canonical condition name (case-sensitive, exactly as in §22). (R-22-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.error_class"></a>

#### error\_class

Which classification range this code belongs to.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.meaning"></a>

#### meaning

One-line meaning of the condition the code signals.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.data_policy"></a>

#### data\_policy

Whether ``error.data`` is spec-normative or sender-defined. (R-22.1-k, R-22.3-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.data_keys"></a>

#### data\_keys

The keys a normative ``data`` payload MUST carry, if any. (R-22.3-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ErrorCodeRegistryEntry.http_status"></a>

#### http\_status

The HTTP status this code maps to on Streamable HTTP. (§22.6)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ERROR_CODE_REGISTRY"></a>

#### ERROR\_CODE\_REGISTRY

The complete §22 error-code registry, keyed by numeric code (§6.5, §22.2, §22.3).
The same ``code`` applies on every transport; ``http_status`` is the Streamable
HTTP overlay (§22.6). ``-32602`` has a single entry even though several distinct
conditions collapse onto it (the specific condition is conveyed by message/data).

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.RESERVED_ERROR_CODES"></a>

#### RESERVED\_ERROR\_CODES

The reserved codes an extension-defined code MUST NOT collide with: the five
standard JSON-RPC codes, the two protocol-specific codes, and ``-32001``. (R-22.7-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.lookup_error_code"></a>

#### lookup\_error\_code

```python
def lookup_error_code(code: int) -> ErrorCodeRegistryEntry | None
```

Look up the registry entry for ``code``, or ``None`` if not in the §22 registry.

An absent entry is not an error — receivers MUST tolerate unknown codes (see
:func:`describe_unknown_error_code`). (R-22.7-e)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.classify_error_code"></a>

#### classify\_error\_code

```python
def classify_error_code(code: int) -> ErrorCodeClass
```

Classify any integer ``code`` into an :class:`ErrorCodeClass`, even unknown codes.

A registry entry's own class always wins; otherwise the code is placed by range: the
server-error sub-range (``-32000..-32099``) → ``SERVER_DEFINED``, any other
reserved-range code → ``JSON_RPC_STANDARD``, everything else → ``EXTENSION_DEFINED``.
(§22.2, §22.7, R-22.7-a)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.is_reserved_error_code"></a>

#### is\_reserved\_error\_code

```python
def is_reserved_error_code(code: int) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``code`` is one of the eight reserved codes. (R-22.7-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.ExtensionCodeValidation"></a>

## ExtensionCodeValidation Objects

```python
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ExtensionCodeValidation()
```

Outcome of :func:`validate_extension_error_code`.

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.validate_extension_error_code"></a>

#### validate\_extension\_error\_code

```python
def validate_extension_error_code(code: object) -> ExtensionCodeValidation
```

Validate that ``code`` is a legal extension-defined error code.

An integer that does not collide with any reserved code. Extensions SHOULD also
carry structured ``data`` (R-22.7-d) — a payload concern, not enforced here.
(R-22.7-a, R-22.7-b, R-22.7-c)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.is_error_code_in_class"></a>

#### is\_error\_code\_in\_class

```python
def is_error_code_in_class(code: object, cls: ErrorCodeClass) -> bool
```

Return ``True`` when ``code`` is allowed for the given classification.

For ``SERVER_DEFINED`` the code MUST lie in ``-32000..-32099``; for
``EXTENSION_DEFINED`` it MUST be a non-reserved integer outside the reserved range;
for the standard/protocol classes it MUST be the corresponding registered code.
(§22.2, §22.7)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.is_valid_error_object"></a>

#### is\_valid\_error\_object

```python
def is_valid_error_object(value: object) -> bool
```

Validate the canonical error-object shape (§22.1).

``code`` present and an integer (possibly negative), ``message`` present and a
string, ``data`` optional. (R-22.1-c, R-22.1-h, R-22.1-i, R-22.1-k, AC-34.6)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.has_exactly_result_or_error"></a>

#### has\_exactly\_result\_or\_error

```python
def has_exactly_result_or_error(response: object) -> bool
```

Validate the mutual-exclusion invariant: exactly one of ``result`` or ``error``.

Never both, never neither. (R-22.1-a, AC-34.1)

<a id="stackific.mcp.protocol.errors.is_valid_error_response"></a>

#### is\_valid\_error\_response

```python
def is_valid_error_response(value: object) -> bool
```

Validate an error response envelope per §22.1/§22.6.
